prompt: Which choice best states the main idea of the passage?

choices.text: Mr. Owusu's attentiveness to customers, not his produce, made his stall special.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage stresses that customers returned because he knew and remembered them.

choices.text: Mr. Owusu's daughter took over the stall and ran it just as he had.
choices.rationale: The daughter notices his gift; the passage does not say she took over.

choices.text: The market declined because several stalls closed at around the same time.
choices.rationale: The passage notes that not a single other stall had closed.

choices.text: Mr. Owusu succeeded mainly because his vegetables cost less than his rivals' did.
choices.rationale: The passage says it was not his prices that set him apart.

explanation: The passage's point is that personal attentiveness, not produce, drew customers back.

stimulus.title: The Stall That Listened
stimulus.text: Every Saturday, Mr. Owusu set out the same vegetables on the same table, and for years his stall was just one among many. What set him apart, his daughter noticed, was not his prices but his memory. He recalled which customer's child disliked onions, who was cooking for a crowd that weekend, who had been ill and needed something gentle. He would set aside a perfect tomato for one regular and toss an extra handful of beans to another. People did not return only for the produce; they returned because, at his stall, they were known. When he finally retired, the market felt larger and lonelier, though not a single other stall had closed.

prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: The high cost of distant spices spurred voyages that eventually made spices common.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage links costly, rare spices to voyages that made them ordinary.

choices.text: Black pepper has always been a cheap and easily available kitchen ingredient.
choices.rationale: The passage says spices were once nearly as valuable as silver.

choices.text: Sailors avoided long voyages because the spice trade was already too crowded.
choices.rationale: The high prices drove sailors to seek new routes, not avoid voyages.

choices.text: European merchants invented spices and were the first ever to harvest them.
choices.rationale: Merchants traded spices; they did not invent or first harvest them.

explanation: The passage traces spices from costly rarity to abundance through new sea routes.

stimulus.title: The Price of Pepper
stimulus.text: There was a time when a handful of black pepper could rival a handful of silver. The spice grew only in distant lands, and by the time it reached a European market it had passed through many hands, each adding a markup. Merchants guarded their routes jealously and spread tall tales about how spices were harvested, partly to discourage rivals. The high prices drove sailors to seek their own sea paths to the source, voyages that redrew the map of the world. In the end, the very abundance those voyages produced made spices ordinary, and the fortunes built on rarity quietly dissolved.

prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: Lichens are the only living things able to survive on exposed rock surfaces.
choices.rationale: The passage does not claim lichens are the only rock survivors.

choices.text: Lichens compete with one another for the limited sunlight on a rock.
choices.rationale: Competition among lichens is not the focus.

choices.text: Lichens prepare bare rock for later plants by helping form the first soil.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage shows lichens building the soil that lets later plants grow.

choices.text: Mosses and small plants arrive on bare rock before any lichen does.
choices.rationale: The passage says lichens come first and mosses follow.

explanation: The passage frames the lichen as a pioneer that makes soil for what comes next.

stimulus.title: First on the Rock
stimulus.text: When a bare rock is left exposed, the first life to arrive is often a lichen, a strange partnership of fungus and alga living as one. The fungus grips the stone and gathers moisture; the alga, sheltered inside, makes food from sunlight. Together they need no soil at all. As a lichen grows, it slowly releases acids that crack the rock surface, and its dead tissue mixes with grit to form the first thin crumbs of soil. Years later, mosses and small plants can take root in that beginning. The lichen will likely be crowded out by the very community it made possible, an unsung founder of the green world that follows.

prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: Scribes abandoned their work as soon as the first printing presses were built.
choices.rationale: It notes scholars still prized handwritten volumes, so scribes did not vanish at once.

choices.text: The price of books dropped instantly once Gutenberg's printing press appeared.
choices.rationale: The passage explicitly says costs did not fall overnight.

choices.text: Gutenberg's reusable metal type made books far easier to reproduce and to spread.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage emphasizes how reusable type transformed the reproduction and reach of books.

choices.text: Handwritten books remained more beautiful and more valued than any printed book.
choices.rationale: Beauty of handwriting is not the passage's claim.

explanation: The passage frames the press as a turning point in how widely texts could circulate.

stimulus.title: Letters of Lead
stimulus.text: Before the 1450s, a book in Europe was copied by hand, a task that could occupy a scribe for a year. Johannes Gutenberg changed that arithmetic. By casting individual letters in metal, he could arrange them into a page, print hundreds of copies, then rearrange the same letters for the next page. The cost of a book did not fall overnight, and many scholars still prized handwritten volumes. But within fifty years, presses across the continent had produced millions of books. Ideas that once crept from city to city now traveled in bulk, and a reader in one town could hold the exact words read by a stranger far away.

prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: Potassium is the single mineral that crops need in order to grow well.
choices.rationale: It names potassium and phosphorus, not a single required mineral.

choices.text: Volcanic ash builds fertile soil, drawing farmers despite the danger of eruptions.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage explains the fertility-versus-danger trade-off that keeps farmers near volcanoes.

choices.text: Farming is effectively impossible anywhere within reach of a volcano.
choices.rationale: The passage says many people do farm there.

choices.text: Volcanoes pose essentially no danger to the villages that sit beside them.
choices.rationale: The passage notes a real danger, so volcanoes are not harmless.

explanation: The passage balances the benefit of fertile soil against eruption risk.

stimulus.title: Gifts of the Volcano
stimulus.text: It seems strange that people would farm in the shadow of a volcano, but many do, and for good reason. When a volcano erupts, it scatters ash rich in minerals such as potassium and phosphorus. Over years, this ash breaks down into some of the most fertile soil on Earth. Crops planted in it can grow unusually well, which is why slopes near certain volcanoes are crowded with farms and villages. The bargain is uneasy: the same mountain that feeds the fields can also threaten them. Farmers weigh the steady gift of rich soil against the rare but real danger overhead, and generation after generation, many choose to stay.

prompt: Which choice best states the main idea of the passage?

choices.text: The tardigrade is the smallest animal that scientists have ever discovered on Earth.
choices.rationale: The passage compares its size to salt but never calls it the smallest animal.

choices.text: The tardigrade can survive only in the vacuum and radiation of outer space.
choices.rationale: Space is one extreme it can survive, not its home.

choices.text: Scientists have now fully explained exactly how the tardigrade protects its cells.
choices.rationale: The text says scientists are 'still untangling' the mechanism.

choices.text: The tardigrade endures extreme conditions by drying into a protected, revivable state.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage centers on how the tardigrade endures extremes by drying out.

explanation: The passage's focus is the dehydration survival strategy and its partial explanation.

stimulus.title: The Survivor
stimulus.text: Smaller than a grain of salt, the tardigrade looks almost comical, plodding on eight stubby legs. Its talent, however, is anything but ordinary. When its surroundings dry out, the creature expels most of its water and curls into a husk called a tun. In this state it can endure boiling heat, the vacuum of space, and doses of radiation that would kill nearly any other animal. Years later, a single drop of water can revive it. Scientists are still untangling how the tardigrade protects its cells, but one clue is a special set of proteins that seem to harden into glass, cradling delicate structures until water returns.

prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: Every white-crowned sparrow sings exactly the same unchanging song everywhere.
choices.rationale: The passage stresses variation, not sameness.

choices.text: Sparrows are born already knowing the full song they will sing as adults.
choices.rationale: Birds must learn the song; they are not born knowing it.

choices.text: Sparrows learn local song dialects that vary by place and shift across generations.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage explains learned, place-based dialects that change over generations.

choices.text: Human accents are largely caused by people imitating nearby birdsong.
choices.rationale: The human-accent comparison is an analogy, not a cause.

explanation: The passage's through-line is learned, drifting song dialects.

stimulus.title: Songs With Accents
stimulus.text: A white-crowned sparrow raised near San Francisco does not sing quite like one raised a hundred miles north. The basic melody is the same, but small flourishes differ from place to place, forming what biologists call song dialects. Young sparrows learn these dialects by listening to adult males during a brief window in their first months. A bird that hears no song in that window grows up to sing a thin, simplified tune. The dialects can drift over generations, much as human accents do, so a recording made decades ago may sound subtly foreign to the birds singing in the same meadow today.

prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: Mathematicians decided that zero should be removed from written calculations.
choices.rationale: Mathematicians embraced zero, rather than removing it.

choices.text: Ancient people were unable to count sheep or coins without using zero.
choices.rationale: The passage says people counted sheep and coins easily without zero.

choices.text: Treating zero as a true number transformed how people could write and calculate.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage shows how recognizing zero as a number reshaped writing and calculation.

choices.text: Zero was always understood as an ordinary number throughout all of history.
choices.rationale: The passage says zero was long absent and unsettling, not always understood.

explanation: The passage's point is that accepting zero as a number transformed mathematics.

stimulus.title: The Number That Was Nothing
stimulus.text: For much of history, there was no symbol for nothing. People counted sheep and coins easily enough, but the idea of writing down an empty amount seemed pointless, even unsettling. The breakthrough came when mathematicians treated zero not as the absence of a number but as a number in its own right, one that could be added, subtracted, and used to hold a place in larger figures. With zero, the difference between 5 and 50 could be written clearly, and long calculations grew far simpler. A concept that once felt like a riddle became the quiet hinge on which much of modern mathematics turns.
